Find Sherpas Boutique Search Agency
Search Quality Report

60 Avoca Searches. 21 Revenue-Risk Moments.

An independent audit of how Avoca search handles product names, brands, filters, and shopper intent.
Prepared by Michal Pekarcik · Find Sherpas

A sample of the 60 real customer queries we tested

scarfs water drop summer scarves sock canvas tote bags barbour scarf food throws & blankets stylish bags blue jellycat bag cook books knit gift for her throws-blankets perfume barb menswear totebags women's fashion jellycat toys stanley bags under 50 purses women's barbour coat need a gift basket with irish food
Report Crossroads

Jump to a section

Search risk at a glance → Tested query patterns → Improvement map → Advanced diagnostics → Prioritized roadmap → Revenue-risk failures → Detailed analysis → Complete test results → Scope and boundaries →
Summary

Search risk at a glance

The short version

We tested 60 realistic customer queries against Avoca's search, live on site. The audit checks whether customers see a relevant top result, not just whether the search engine returns something.

This report focuses on the 21 queries where a customer likely hits a dead end: an off-topic result, a missing interpretation, a dropped constraint, or a relevant product buried too far down.

The first fix to ship: Add an intent & boost layer before keyword ranking (brand, product-type, price, and bestseller signals) — This addresses the core ranking failures affecting Product Discovery, Typo Tolerance, Language Understanding, Shopping Context, and Filters & Constraints capabilities.

35%
revenue risk. 21 of 60 tested queries cause a likely customer bounce today. These are queries where a customer sees a dead end: no relevant top result, wrong interpretation, or the right product buried too far down.
21
Revenue risk
Off-topic top result, missing interpretation, or severe ranking failure. The customer leaves.
34
Friction
The query is close, but still creates work: the best match is not surfaced cleanly enough.
5
Working well
Top result is relevant and ranking is near-optimal. No immediate attention needed.
68%
Right products found, ranked too low
#4
Avg. best result position
38%
Best result outside top 3
25%
Irrelevant #1 result
Most severe example A customer searching "jellycat toys" sees "Smudge Elephant Blankie" at the #1 spot — not what they asked for.
Pattern Calibration Map

Where each query family needs tuning

These are diagnostic probes, not grades. Each row shows the strength of the tuning signal in that sample, separating severe shopper-visible misses from minor ranking polish.

Product Discovery
Direct match
Exact-match polish
4 probes reviewed: 3 polish notes, 1 stable result.
The pattern mostly surfaces relevant products; the remaining work is ranking polish.
Example “matching mugs & notebooks” — The search returned 3 relevant mugs but ranked them incorrectly by relevance score.
Can customers find an exact product when they already know the name?
Next tuning move: Protect exact product-title matches and verify they are not outranked by broader keyword matches.
Product Discovery
Broad category
Collection routing
4 probes reviewed: 1 visible miss, 1 friction point, 2 polish notes.
At least one probe showed a shopper-visible miss. The example below is the clearest case.
Example “knitwear” — The highest relevance result 'Poinciana Georgina Knit Jumper' (0.688 relevance) was buried at position #11, while lower-relevance items like 'Light Blue…
Does search understand high-level shopping terms and collection-style queries?
Next tuning move: Map common retail terms to collections, category pages, and merchandising rules.
Typo Tolerance
Typo
Typo recovery
4 probes reviewed: 2 visible misses, 1 friction point, 1 polish note.
At least one probe showed a shopper-visible miss. The example below is the clearest case.
Example “scarfs” — The search engine successfully handled the typo 'scarfs' and retrieved all relevant scarf products, but severely misranked them.
Does search recover when brand or product terms are misspelled?
Next tuning move: Add fuzzy matching for high-value brands, product names, and common category terms.
Typo Tolerance
Merged words
Pattern tuning
4 probes reviewed: 4 visible misses.
At least one probe showed a shopper-visible miss. The example below is the clearest case.
Example “totebags” — The search engine correctly identified tote bag products but ranked them terribly.
This probe checks whether search handles this customer query pattern predictably.
Next tuning move: Review the failed examples and add this pattern to the search relevance regression set.
Typo Tolerance
Split word
Pattern tuning
4 probes reviewed: 2 friction points, 1 polish note, 1 stable result.
The pattern works in places, but the experience is not consistently clean.
Example “hair accessories” — The search engine correctly retrieved relevant hair accessories but ranked them poorly.
This probe checks whether search handles this customer query pattern predictably.
Next tuning move: Review the failed examples and add this pattern to the search relevance regression set.
Typo Tolerance
Special character
Pattern tuning
4 probes reviewed: 3 visible misses, 1 polish note.
At least one probe showed a shopper-visible miss. The example below is the clearest case.
Example “women's fashion & accessories” — The highest relevance result 'Mini Floral Navy Cotton Hair Band' (0.621 relevance) was buried at position #15, while much lower relevance items like 'Derrybeg…
This probe checks whether search handles this customer query pattern predictably.
Next tuning move: Review the failed examples and add this pattern to the search relevance regression set.
Typo Tolerance
Partial query
Autocomplete intent
4 probes reviewed: 1 friction point, 3 polish notes.
The pattern works in places, but the experience is not consistently clean.
Example “barb” — The highest relevance result 'Classic Tartan Barbour Handbag Umbrella' (0.463) was ranked #6 instead of #1.
Do incomplete typed-ahead queries surface plausible products before the full term is entered?
Next tuning move: Tune prefix matching and autocomplete-backed retrieval for short or incomplete terms.
Language Understanding
Synonym
Vocabulary mapping
4 probes reviewed: 1 visible miss, 2 polish notes, 1 stable result.
At least one probe showed a shopper-visible miss. The example below is the clearest case.
Example “gift baskets” — The search engine correctly identified relevant gift sets but ranked them poorly.
Does search connect customer vocabulary to the store's preferred product wording?
Next tuning move: Expand synonym dictionaries and semantic embeddings for the product language customers actually use.
Language Understanding
Natural language
Intent translation
3 probes reviewed: 3 visible misses.
At least one probe showed a shopper-visible miss. The example below is the clearest case.
Example “looking for stylish men's clothing” — Customer searched for 'stylish men's clothing' but got women's jewelry and accessories instead.
Can longer, conversational needs be translated into product intent?
Next tuning move: Extract intent phrases from longer queries before ranking products.
Shopping Context
Plural / singular
Term normalization
4 probes reviewed: 2 friction points, 2 polish notes.
The pattern works in places, but the experience is not consistently clean.
Example “sock” — The search correctly retrieved all sock products, but the ranking is severely misaligned with relevance scores.
Does search normalize simple wording variants such as singular and plural terms?
Next tuning move: Normalize singular, plural, and close inflection variants before retrieval.
Shopping Context
Use case
Pattern tuning
4 probes reviewed: 2 visible misses, 1 friction point, 1 polish note.
At least one probe showed a shopper-visible miss. The example below is the clearest case.
Example “cozy home items” — The query 'cozy home items' should return throws, blankets, and home fragrance products, but instead returned jewelry bracelets in positions #1, #2, #4, and…
This probe checks whether search handles this customer query pattern predictably.
Next tuning move: Review the failed examples and add this pattern to the search relevance regression set.
Shopping Context
Subjective attribute
Merchandising language
3 probes reviewed: 2 visible misses, 1 friction point.
At least one probe showed a shopper-visible miss. The example below is the clearest case.
Example “stylish bags” — The highest relevance result 'Tan Stella Suede Bag' (0.633 relevance) was buried at position #11, while lower-relevance items like 'Black Shoes Drawstring…
Can descriptive shopping language like premium, casual, or comfortable be mapped sensibly?
Next tuning move: Connect subjective descriptors to curated tags, product copy, and merchandising attributes.
Shopping Context
Seasonal / occasion
Occasion matching
3 probes reviewed: 2 friction points, 1 stable result.
The pattern works in places, but the experience is not consistently clean.
Example “winter knitwear” — The three highest relevance products (Olive Mainland Knitted Cardigan 0.535, Storm Melange Damsville Pullover 0.535, Poinciana Georgina Knit Jumper 0.535) were…
Can seasonal or occasion-based needs map to relevant categories and products?
Next tuning move: Add occasion and season tags, then use them as ranking signals for broad needs.
Filters & Constraints
Multi-attribute
Constraint stacking
4 probes reviewed: 1 friction point, 2 polish notes, 1 stable result.
The pattern works in places, but the experience is not consistently clean.
Example “women's barbour coat” — The search correctly retrieved relevant Barbour women's coats, but the ranking is suboptimal.
Can search keep multiple requirements active at the same time?
Next tuning move: Score each constraint independently so one matched term does not drown out the rest.
Filters & Constraints
Price anchored
Price parsing
3 probes reviewed: 3 visible misses.
At least one probe showed a shopper-visible miss. The example below is the clearest case.
Example “knitwear under 100” — The query 'knitwear under 100' specified both a product category (knitwear) and a price constraint (under €100), but the search engine completely ignored both…
Can price constraints be recognized and used as a ranking or filtering signal?
Next tuning move: Parse numeric price limits and connect them to product price metadata.
Brand & Model Search
Brand search
Brand protection
4 probes reviewed: 3 friction points, 1 polish note.
The pattern works in places, but the experience is not consistently clean.
Example “barbour coats” — The highest relevance result 'Sand Liddesdale Quilted Coat' (0.793 relevance) was ranked #7 instead of #1.
Are brand requests recognized and protected from unrelated results?
Next tuning move: Detect brand entities and boost or filter to products from the requested brand.

Advanced Diagnostics

What the visible result set reveals

These checks stay inside the same query → results → audit boundary, but look beyond the single best result.

Result Set Purity

This checks how much of the visible result set is actually relevant, not just whether one good result exists somewhere. A noisy top 5 means customers must visually filter around weak results even when search technically found a match.

32%
top 5 relevant

Result

The visible result set is noisy: only 32% of top 5 results were relevant on average, and top 10 purity was 29%.

Evidence

  • "matching mugs & notebooks": 0/3 relevant in top 5
  • "sleep & loungewear": 0/5 relevant in top 5
  • "food": 0/5 relevant in top 5
  • "knitwear": 0/5 relevant in top 5

How to remedy it

Tune ranking to demote low-relevance partial matches once a strong intent match exists. Add a top-N quality guardrail: for each important query class, require a minimum share of relevant results in the top 5, not only a good best-result position.

Top Result Trust

This asks whether the first result is defensible as the best thing to show a customer. A bad #1 is the clearest search failure because it shapes the shopper's first impression.

67%
questionable #1 results

Result

The first result is not reliable enough: 40 of 60 queries (67%) had a weak, partial, or clearly beaten #1 result.

Evidence

  • "throws-blankets": #1 was "Daffodil Jellybean Joy Pop Bracelet" (score 0.20). The first result is weakly related to the query.
  • "looking for stylish men's clothing": #1 was "Daffodil Jellybean Joy Pop Bracelet" (score 0.21). The first result is weakly related to the query.
  • "totebags": #1 was "Daffodil Jellybean Joy Pop Bracelet" (score 0.22). The first result is weakly related to the query.
  • "menswear": #1 was "Daffodil Jellybean Joy Pop Bracelet" (score 0.24). The first result is weakly related to the query.

How to remedy it

Add a stricter first-result threshold for high-intent queries. If the top result is weak and a stronger result exists lower, boost the stronger exact/category/brand match or suppress the weak partial match from rank #1.

Attribute Drift

This decomposes multi-attribute queries to see which terms survive into the top results. It distinguishes right-category-but-wrong-attribute failures from total search failures.

7
queries with attribute loss

Result

7 multi-attribute queries showed some attribute loss in the top 5. The audit separates preserved, partially preserved, and dropped terms so the team can see which part of the query is being lost.

Evidence

  • "bags under 50": dropped bags, 50; partially preserved none
  • "knitwear under 100": dropped knitwear, 100; partially preserved none
  • "blue jellycat bag": dropped blue, jellycat; partially preserved bag
  • "scarves under 30": dropped scarve; partially preserved 30

How to remedy it

Extract query attributes before ranking and give required attributes explicit weight. For brand, size, material, activity, and style terms, penalize results that match the product type but omit or contradict the attribute.

Diversity vs Redundancy

This checks whether broad or exploratory queries show useful choice, or whether the top results are flooded by repeated variants. It is most useful for multi-brand or broad-catalog ecommerce, and less useful for true single-brand stores.

10
unique families in worst broad top 10

Result

The most redundant broad result set was "beauty": 10 unique product families across 10 top results.

Evidence

  • "beauty": 10 unique product families across 10 top results
  • "food": 10 unique product families across 10 top results
  • "home": 10 unique product families across 10 top results
  • "knitwear": 10 unique product families across 10 top results

How to remedy it

Group color/size/variant duplicates under a product-family cap for broad queries. Keep exact product searches variant-rich, but diversify broad discovery queries across families, brands, styles, and use cases.

Recommendations

Prioritized Roadmap

1

Add an intent & boost layer before keyword ranking (brand, product-type, price, and bestseller signals)

This addresses the core ranking failures affecting Product Discovery, Typo Tolerance, Language Understanding, Shopping Context, and Filters & Constraints capabilities.

Critical Quick Win
2

Implement price constraint parsing and filtering

Enables the search to recognize and apply price limits from natural language queries like "under 50" or "below 100" for Shopping Context capability.

Critical Medium Effort
3

Add compound word splitting for merged terms

Allows recognition of queries like "totebags," "menswear," "homefragrance," and "greetingcards" to improve Typo Tolerance capability.

Critical Medium Effort
4

Build fuzzy matching for common typos and brand names

Handles misspellings like "knitware" for "knitwear" and "jellyact" for "Jellycat" to strengthen Typo Tolerance capability.

Critical Medium Effort
5

Create semantic understanding for natural language queries

Maps conceptual searches like "something warm and cozy" or "stylish men's clothing" to appropriate product categories for Language Understanding capability.

Critical Major Project
6

Add special character normalization

Treats hyphens, ampersands, and apostrophes consistently so "throws-blankets" works the same as "throws & blankets" for Filters & Constraints capability.

Moderate Quick Win
7

Implement category-aware search for broad terms

Ensures searches for "office accessories" or "cozy home items" return products from relevant categories rather than literal keyword matches for Shopping Context capability.

Moderate Medium Effort
Industry Context

How You Compare

Metric Your Score Industry Target Source
Relevant result in top 3 62% 80%+ Baymard Institute
Average best result position #4 #1–2 Industry best practice
Irrelevant #1 result 25% <10% Industry best practice

70% of ecommerce sites cannot handle typos in their site search (Baymard Institute). Sites that do handle them gain a significant edge.

~30% of ecommerce visitors use site search, but they generate a disproportionate share of revenue — search users convert at 1.8–3x the rate of browse-only visitors (Econsultancy, Algolia).

31% of site searches end at a dead end — irrelevant results or zero results — across the average ecommerce site (Baymard Institute).

The top 3 results capture ~55–60% of all clicks. Results below position 3 are seen by a rapidly shrinking share of customers.

Revenue-Risk Failures

The queries most likely to cost sales

These examples show where the customer either sees no useful result, sees the wrong intent first, or has to dig past weaker results to find the right product.

#1 women's fashion & accessories Poor ranking

The best matching result appeared at position #15, after weaker results.

Evidence

The highest relevance result 'Mini Floral Navy Cotton Hair Band' (0.621 relevance) was buried at position #15, while much lower relevance items like 'Derrybeg & Ballybofey Women's Donegal Wool Socks' (0.551 relevance) ranked #1. The top 3 positions contained items with relevance scores of 0.551, 0.555, and 0.555, while items with scores of 0.621, 0.598, and 0.578 were ranked #15, #11, and #12 respectively.

Fix

Re-rank returned products so the strongest intent matches appear first, especially when the right products already exist in the result set.

Customer saw

#1 0.55

Derrybeg & Ballybofey Women's Donegal Wool Socks €16.95

#2 0.55

Gild & Loughlin Women's Donegal Wool Socks €16.95

#3 0.55

Dusty Pink & Portnoo Women's Donegal Wool Socks €16.95

#4 0.56

Gold Elni Sunglasses €17.48

#5 0.45

Yellow 012 Small Umbrella €42.50

Should have seen

#15 0.62

Mini Floral Navy Cotton Hair Band €11.16

#11 0.60

Green Wave Cotton Hair Band €11.16

#12 0.58

Green Wave Cotton Hair Scrunchie €7.96

#8 0.57

Gardenia Tartan Barbour Handbag Umbrella €44.99

#7 0.57

Classic Tartan Barbour Handbag Umbrella €39.99

#2 mugs & notebooks Poor ranking

The best matching result appeared at position #14, after weaker results.

Evidence

The highest relevance result 'Royal Multi Pink Small Mugs - Set of 2' (0.555 relevance) was buried at position #14, while lower-scoring notebooks dominated the top positions. The #1 result 'Yellow 012 Large Dotted Notebook' had only 0.535 relevance, and 8 out of the top 10 results were notebooks despite mugs having higher individual relevance scores.

Fix

Re-rank returned products so the strongest intent matches appear first, especially when the right products already exist in the result set.

#3 scarfs Poor ranking

The best matching result appeared at position #13, after weaker results.

Evidence

The search engine successfully handled the typo 'scarfs' and retrieved all relevant scarf products, but severely misranked them. The highest relevance result 'WR81 Wide Merino Scarf' (0.770) was buried at position #13, while lower-scoring items like 'Poinsettia Larisa Amour Football Scarf' (0.711) appeared at position #2. Multiple high-relevance items were displaced: 'Black Amara Knit Scarf' (0.762) at position #8, 'Prospect Road 100% Silk Scarf' (0.762) at position #11, and 'Pink & Red Checkers 100% Silk Scarf' (0.758) at position #15.

Fix

Re-rank returned products so the strongest intent matches appear first, especially when the right products already exist in the result set.

Customer saw

#1 0.77

Solstice Merino Scarf €55.00

#2 0.71

Poinsettia Larisa Amour Football Scarf €39.99

#3 0.77

Rome Merino Scarf €55.00

#4 0.76

Denim Merino Scarf €55.00

#5 0.73

Dark Navy Check Blue Phia Scarf €39.95

Should have seen

#13 0.77

WR81 Wide Merino Scarf €99.95

#1 0.77

Solstice Merino Scarf €55.00

#3 0.77

Rome Merino Scarf €55.00

#8 0.76

Black Amara Knit Scarf €70.00

#11 0.76

Prospect Road 100% Silk Scarf €75.00

#4 looking for stylish men's clothing No semantic understanding

The best matching result appeared at position #12, after weaker results.

Evidence

Customer searched for 'stylish men's clothing' but got women's jewelry and accessories instead. Top results include 'Daffodil Jellybean Joy Pop Bracelet' (#1), 'Limoncello Pearly Queen Dream Bracelet' (#2), and 'Petal Pink Tilly Metallic Mini Dress' (#6). The only item that could remotely be considered men's clothing is 'Blue Dad Cherish Stoneware Mug' (#8), which isn't clothing at all. The search engine completely failed to understand that the customer wanted men's apparel and instead returned random products with no connection to menswear.

Fix

Add synonym and semantic mappings for this customer phrasing so the engine recognizes the intended product need.

#1 0.21

Daffodil Jellybean Joy Pop Bracelet €55.00

Fuchsia Forest Friends Quilted Cotton Tote Bag €65.00

#6 0.25

#5 knitwear Poor ranking

The best matching result appeared at position #11, after weaker results.

Evidence

The highest relevance result 'Poinciana Georgina Knit Jumper' (0.688 relevance) was buried at position #11, while lower-relevance items like 'Light Blue Federica Cardigan' (0.594) and 'Rose Vitow Pullover' (0.520) occupied the top positions. The second-best result 'Olive Mainland Knitted Cardigan' (0.656 relevance) was at position #6. Additionally, very low-relevance items like 'Créme Anaphe Button Vest' (0.395) and 'Birch Vita Textured Waistcoat' (0.383) appeared in positions #7 and #13 respectively.

Fix

Re-rank returned products so the strongest intent matches appear first, especially when the right products already exist in the result set.

Detailed Analysis

Product Discovery

5 examples
"knitwear"
Broad category · Critical · Best result at position #11

The highest relevance result 'Poinciana Georgina Knit Jumper' (0.688 relevance) was buried at position #11, while lower-relevance items like 'Light Blue Federica Cardigan' (0.594) and 'Rose Vitow Pullover' (0.520) occupied the top positions. The second-best result 'Olive Mainland Knitted Cardigan' (0.656 relevance) was at position #6. Additionally, very low-relevance items like 'Créme Anaphe Button Vest' (0.395) and 'Birch Vita Textured Waistcoat' (0.383) appeared in positions #7 and #13 respectively.

Customer Saw

  1. Light Blue Federica Cardigan (€89.00)
  2. Rose Vitow Pullover (€175.00)
  3. Heather Zendaya Cardigan (€189.95)
  4. White Vito Cardigan (€175.00)
  5. Strawberry Damsville Pullover (€110.00)

Should Have Seen

  1. Poinciana Georgina Knit Jumper (€155.00)
  2. Olive Mainland Knitted Cardigan (€101.94)
  3. Light Blue Federica Cardigan (€89.00)

Best match was buried at position #11.

"beauty"
Broad category · Moderate · Best result at position #1

The search correctly retrieved beauty products but ranked them poorly. High-relevance items like 'Nutriplenish Overnight Serum' (0.535 relevance) appeared at position #12, 'Cherry Almond Shampoo' (0.523 relevance) at position #13, and 'The Spark Shampoo' (0.512 relevance) at position #15. Meanwhile, lower-relevance items like 'Thickening Tonic' (0.479 relevance) ranked at position #4.

Customer Saw

  1. Beauty Elixir Face Mist (€41.00)
  2. Shampure Shampoo (€25.00)
  3. Geranium Shampoo (€30.00)
  4. Thickening Tonic (€31.00)
  5. Geranium Conditioner (€30.00)

Should Have Seen

  1. Beauty Elixir Face Mist (€41.00)
  2. Nutriplenish Overnight Serum (€50.00)
  3. Geranium Shampoo (€30.00)
"matching mugs & notebooks"
Direct match · Minor · Best result at position #3

The search returned 3 relevant mugs but ranked them incorrectly by relevance score. The highest scoring result 'Fuchsia Forest Friends Mug' (0.379) was placed at position #3, while lower scoring results 'Red Stars Mug' (0.365) and 'Blue 'Dad' Cherish Stoneware Mug' (0.344) were ranked #1 and #2 respectively.

Customer Saw

  1. Red Stars Mug (€14.95)
  2. Blue 'Dad' Cherish Stoneware Mug (€19.95)
  3. Fuchsia Forest Friends Mug (€14.95)

Should Have Seen

  1. Fuchsia Forest Friends Mug (€14.95)
  2. Red Stars Mug (€14.95)
  3. Blue 'Dad' Cherish Stoneware Mug (€19.95)
"sleep & loungewear"
Direct match · Minor · Best result at position #3

All results are relevant sleep & loungewear products (pyjama sets and slippers), but the ranking is suboptimal. The highest relevance item 'Green Wave Cotton Pyjama Set' (0.566) appears at position #3 instead of #1, while lower-scoring items like 'Pink Tulip Cotton Pyjama Set' (0.555) rank first.

Customer Saw

  1. Pink Tulip Cotton Pyjama Set (€63.96)
  2. Orange Rose Cotton Pyjama Set (€63.96)
  3. Green Wave Cotton Pyjama Set (€63.96)
  4. Mini Floral Navy Cotton Pyjama Set (€63.96)
  5. Pink Wavy Bow Satin Pyjama Set (€85.00)

Should Have Seen

  1. Green Wave Cotton Pyjama Set (€63.96)
  2. Orange Rose Cotton Pyjama Set (€63.96)
  3. Pink Wavy Bow Satin Pyjama Set (€85.00)
"throws & blankets"
Direct match · Minor · Best result at position #1

The search correctly retrieved relevant throws and blankets, but the ranking algorithm failed to properly order results by relevance score. Most notably, 'Miners Mohair Throw' with a relevance score of 0.688 (tied for highest) was ranked at position #15 instead of position #4 where it belongs. Additionally, several other high-scoring items were misplaced: 'Bloom Lambswool Throw' (0.676) at #7 instead of #5, 'Pioneer Lambswool Throw' (0.676) at #11 instead of #6, and multiple items with 0.672 scores scattered throughout positions #9-12 instead of being grouped together in positions #7-9.

Customer Saw

  1. WR81 Lambswool Throw (€115.00)

Special character · Critical · Best result at position #15

Language Understanding

5 examples
"looking for stylish men's clothing"
Natural language · Critical · Best result at position #12

Customer searched for 'stylish men's clothing' but got women's jewelry and accessories instead. Top results include 'Daffodil Jellybean Joy Pop Bracelet' (#1), 'Limoncello Pearly Queen Dream Bracelet' (#2), and 'Petal Pink Tilly Metallic Mini Dress' (#6). The only item that could remotely be considered men's clothing is 'Blue Dad Cherish Stoneware Mug' (#8), which isn't clothing at all. The search engine completely failed to understand that the customer wanted men's apparel and instead returned random products with no connection to menswear.

The engine failed to understand that 'warm and cozy' refers to comfort textiles and 'irish mill' refers to traditional mill-woven heritage products.

Filters & Constraints

"knitwear under 100"
Price anchored · Critical · Best result at position #6

The query 'knitwear under 100' specified both a product category (knitwear) and a price constraint (under €100), but the search engine completely ignored both constraints. Zero knitwear items appear in the results - instead showing bracelets (#1, #2, #4, #11), mugs (#5, #8, #10), a hamper (#3), a dress (#6), and tote bags (#7, #9, #12). All results are under €100, but none are knitwear products.

Positive Findings

What's Working

  • Retrieval is working — relevant results were found in 26 of 60 queries. The primary issue is ranking, not finding: the right results exist in the result set but aren't being surfaced at the top.
  • Close to correct on 32 queries ("throws & blankets", "matching mugs & notebooks", "sleep & loungewear"): the best result was within the top 3, just not at #1.

55 frag Partial query Minor #1 Poor ranking
56 canvas tote bags Direct match Pass #1 —
57 sleep wear Split word Pass #1 —
58 sweaters Synonym Pass #1 —

Scope and Boundaries

What this audit covers

Included

Search relevance, ranking quality, retrieval gaps, query interpretation failures, visible result ordering, and prioritized fixes based on the tested query set.

Not included

Implementation work, platform configuration changes, merchandising strategy, analytics instrumentation, or a full conversion-rate analysis beyond the search result experience.

Next Steps

We'd welcome the opportunity to walk you through these findings in detail and discuss a remediation plan tailored to your team's priorities and timeline.

The issues identified in this audit are fixable — most ecommerce search platforms support the capabilities tested here, and the roadmap above is ordered by impact to help you prioritize.
